sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "God Did Not Reject His People"

heb.paragraph: Leimma in v. 5 (‘a remnant chosen by grace’) evokes the OT remnant theology running from Isaiah 10:22 through Amos, Micah, and Zephaniah. The remnant proves that God has not totally abandoned Israel. Even in Elijah’s time, God preserved seven thousand (v. 4, citing 1 Kgs 19:18). The remnant exists by grace (kata charin), not by works — a qualification Paul insists on in v. 6.

panels.ctx: Romans 11 answers the question raised by chapter 9–10: has God rejected Israel? Paul’s answer is an emphatic mē genoito (v. 1). He offers three reasons: (1) a remnant exists now, including Paul himself (vv. 1–6); (2) Israel’s hardening serves to bring salvation to the Gentiles (vv. 7–16); and (3) Israel will ultimately be saved (vv. 25–32). The olive tree metaphor (vv. 16–24) is Paul’s most vivid illustration of the relationship between Israel and the church: Gentile believers are grafted into Israel’s olive tree, not the other way around. The church does not replace Israel but is incorporated into Israel’s covenant story.

cross.note: God reserved seven thousand in Israel who had not bowed to Baal — the remnant Paul cites.
Isaiah’s remnant prophecy: only a remnant of Israel will return.
Paul cites Isaiah’s ‘spirit of stupor’ as God’s judicial hardening of Israel.

notes.note: Paul cites himself as proof that God has not rejected Israel: he is an Israelite, of the tribe of Benjamin. The remnant is living evidence of God’s continuing faithfulness.
The remnant is chosen by grace. Paul adds the essential qualification: if by grace, then not by works; if by works, grace is no longer grace. The categories are mutually exclusive.
The rest were hardened. Paul cites Deuteronomy 29:4, Isaiah 29:10, and Psalm 69:22–23 to show that Israel’s hardening is scripturally anticipated and judicially imposed.
Israel’s stumbling is not final but purposeful: it led to Gentile salvation, which will in turn provoke Israel to jealousy. The logic is redemptive, not punitive.
Calvin affirms that God’s foreknowledge of his people guarantees their preservation. The remnant exists because God will not abandon those he has chosen from before the foundation of the world.
Calvin’s famous insistence: grace and works are contradictory principles. If the remnant is chosen by grace, works are excluded entirely. Any admixture of works negates grace.
The verb proginōskō (‘foreknew’) here, as in 8:29, carries the sense of relational foreknowledge: God set his covenant love on Israel beforehand. This is not mere prescience but elective love.
The ‘spirit of stupor’ (pneuma katanyxeōs) combines Isaiah 29:10 with Deuteronomy 29:4. The compound quotation shows that Israel’s blindness was prophetically anticipated.

Moo reads this section as establishing two complementary truths: God has preserved a remnant within Israel (vv. 1 6), and Israel’s hardening serves a redemptive purpose for the Gentiles (vv. 7–16). Neither truth negates the other.
Schreiner stresses that the remnant’s existence demonstrates God’s faithfulness to his covenant. The remnant is not a consolation prize but proof that God’s word has not failed (9:6).

Schreiner argues that Paul envisions a great eschatological reversal: Israel’s failure leads to Gentile inclusion, which leads to Israel’s jealousy, which leads to Israel’s restoration, which will be ‘life from the dead.’

heb.paragraph: Mystērion in v. 25 denotes a truth previously hidden but now revealed by God. The ‘mystery’ Paul discloses is that Israel’s hardening is partial (not total) and temporary (until the full number of Gentiles comes in). After the Gentile fullness, ‘all Israel will be saved.’ The term carries the sense of a divine plan that transcends human reasoning and can only be known through revelation.

Anexereunētos (v. 33) describes God’s judgments as beyond human investigation. The doxology of vv. 33–36 is Paul’s response to the mystery he has just revealed: confronted with God’s inscrutable wisdom, the only appropriate response is worship, not comprehension.

panels.ctx: Romans 11:17–36 contains Paul’s olive tree metaphor and his climactic declaration about Israel’s future. The olive tree represents the people of God rooted in the patriarchal promises. Natural branches (unbelieving Jews) have been broken off, and wild branches (Gentile believers) grafted in. Paul warns Gentile Christians against arrogance: they stand by faith and can be cut off (v. 22). The mystery of v. 25 — partial hardening until the Gentile fullness, then all Israel saved — has generated centuries of debate. Who is ‘all Israel’? Ethnic Israel, spiritual Israel, or all the elect? The most natural reading, supported by the context of chapters 9–11, is ethnic Israel as a whole, saved eschatologically through faith in Christ. The doxology of vv. 33–36 crowns the entire argument of chapters 1–11.

cross.note: God called Israel a ‘thriving olive tree’ — the OT background for Paul’s metaphor.
The Redeemer will come to Zion and remove ungodliness from Jacob — the prophecy Paul cites in v. 26.
Who has known the mind of the Lord? — the source of Paul’s doxology in vv. 34–35.
Who has ever given to God that God should repay? — Paul’s final quotation in the doxology.

notes.note: The olive tree metaphor warns Gentile believers against anti-Jewish arrogance. The Gentile church did not replace Israel but was grafted into Israel’s covenant. The root supports the branches, not vice versa.
The mystery: Israel’s hardening is temporary, lasting only until the fullness of the Gentiles. Then ‘all Israel will be saved’ — a national turning to Christ at the end of history.
Israel is simultaneously ‘enemies’ regarding the gospel and ‘beloved’ regarding election. God’s gifts and his call are irrevocable (ametamelēta).
The doxology that crowns the argument. God’s wisdom, knowledge, judgments, and paths are beyond human comprehension. From him, through him, and to him are all things. To him be glory forever.

Calvin interprets ‘all Israel’ as the totality of God’s elect from both Jews and Gentiles — the full Israel of God. This reading is contested but reflects Calvin’s ecclesiology in which the church is the true Israel.

Calvin sees the doxology as the proper conclusion to the mystery of predestination: when human reason reaches its limits, worship begins. The incomprehensibility of God’s ways is not a deficiency but a glory.

The term agrielaios (‘wild olive’) is significant: wild olive branches do not naturally produce good fruit. The grafting of wild branches into a cultivated tree is ‘contrary to nature’ (v. 24), emphasising the supernatural character of Gentile inclusion.
The phrase ‘all Israel’ (pas Israēl) has multiple interpretations. The NET favours ethnic Israel as a whole, noting that ‘Israel’ refers to ethnic Israel throughout chapters 9–11 without exception.

Moo argues that the olive tree represents the one people of God across salvation history. Gentiles are not a new people but participants in an existing covenant story. The warning against arrogance (v. 20) implies that faith, not ethnicity, is the criterion for standing.

Moo takes ‘all Israel’ as ethnic Israel in its corporate entirety, saved through faith in Christ at or near the parousia. The mystery is not that some Jews will be saved but that the nation as a whole will turn to Christ.

Moo reads the doxology as the climax not merely of chapters 9–11 but of the entire doctrinal section (chs. 1–11). The transition from theology to doxology is paradigmatic: the proper response to God’s sovereign mercy is worship.

Schreiner takes ‘all Israel’ as ethnic Israel and reads the mystery as a genuine eschatological prediction: there will be a mass turning of ethnic Jews to Christ before the end. This does not require every individual Jew to be saved but the nation as a whole.

Schreiner argues that the doxology is the fitting response to the tension between sovereignty and mercy that has driven chapters 9–11. The human mind cannot resolve the tension; it can only worship the God who holds both together.

themes.note: Covenant peaks at 10: the entire chapter concerns God’s covenant faithfulness to Israel. Sovereignty at 9 reflects the olive tree metaphor and the mystery of divine election. Prophecy at 8 captures the ‘all Israel will be saved’ prediction.

rows.label: vv.33–36
rows.text: Doxology: The Depth of God’s Wisdom

lit.note: Romans 11 resolves the Israel question: God has not rejected his people. A remnant exists now, hardening serves Gentile inclusion, and all Israel will ultimately be saved. The doxology crowns the entire doctrinal argument of chapters 1–11.

hebtext.note: Leimma in v. 5 (‘a remnant chosen by grace’) evokes the OT remnant theology running from Isaiah 10:22 through Amos, Micah, and Zephaniah. The remnant proves that God has not totally abandoned Israel. Even in Elijah’s time, God preserved seven thousand (v. 4, citing 1 Kgs 19:18). The remnant exists by grace (kata charin), not by works — a qualification Paul insists on in v. 6.

Mystērion in v. 25 denotes a truth previously hidden but now revealed by God. The ‘mystery’ Paul discloses is that Israel’s hardening is partial (not total) and temporary (until the full number of Gentiles comes in). After the Gentile fullness, ‘all Israel will be saved.’ The term carries the sense of a divine plan that transcends human reasoning and can only be known through revelation.

Anexereunētos (v. 33) describes God’s judgments as beyond human investigation. The doxology of vv. 33–36 is Paul’s response to the mystery he has just revealed: confronted with God’s inscrutable wisdom, the only appropriate response is worship, not comprehension.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Olive Tree: Israel’s Future

translations.text: What then? What the people of Israel sought so earnestly they did not obtain. The elect among them did, but the others were hardened,
What then? Israel failed to obtain what it was seeking. The elect obtained it, but the rest were hardened,
I ask then: Did God reject his people? By no means! I am an Israelite myself, a descendant of Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin.
I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means! For I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham, a member of the tribe of Benjamin.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul cites himself as proof that God has not rejected Israel: he is an Israelite, of the tribe of Benjamin. The remnant is living evidence of God’s continuing faithfulness."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin affirms that God’s foreknowledge of his people guarantees their preservation. The remnant exists because God will not abandon those he has chosen from before the foundation of the world."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The verb proginōskō (‘foreknew’) here, as in 8:29, carries the sense of relational foreknowledge: God set his covenant love on Israel beforehand. This is not mere prescience but elective love."

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Olive Tree within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
